It uses masked embeddings to realize variable sizes, augments the policy state with popularity, current size, and a novel per-entity performance ratio indicator, and designs a reward that balances recommendation quality with a squared memory-cost term to encourage early pruning then fine-grained tuning.
paper_title: Continuous Input Embedding Size Search For Recommender Systems

problem: In a large continuous action space, value estimation by critics is noisy and exhaustive greedy exploration is computationally prohibitive, leading to suboptimal convergence.
method: Random walk-based exploration around the actor’s proposed size: construct a neighborhood graph over integer sizes, perform short random walks to sample a small set of nearby candidate sizes, evaluate them via the critic, and pick the best Q-valued action. Combined with mild Gaussian noise, this efficiently broadens exploration and stabilizes convergence.
paper_title: Continuous Input Embedding Size Search For Recommender Systems

problem: Embedding-size decisions obtained during RL search may not be robust because the base recommender isn’t fully converged each iteration, and different memory budgets may be required.
method: Selective retraining under a target sparsity: maintain the top-l embedding masks found during search, fully retrain the base model for each, and choose the best-performing mask. The scheme can be extended to multiple sparsity targets in one shot for budget-flexible deployment.
paper_title: Continuous Input Embedding Size Search For Recommender Systems

problem: Conversational dense retrievers trained on fixed, sparse multi-turn data fail to generalize to diverse conversation forms and subtle intent variations.
method: CONVAUG: an LLM-driven, multi-level augmentation suite that creates positive and hard-negative conversations at token (random masking; entity replacement for negatives), turn (dependency-aware masking/reordering via an LLM-inferred turn-dependency DAG; insertion of on-topic noisy turns), and conversation levels (paraphrasing; intent shifting for negatives) to expose the encoder to varied expressions while preserving or purposefully altering intent.
paper_title: Generalizing Conversational Dense Retrieval via LLM-Cognition Data Augmentation

problem: LLM-based augmentations often contain hallucinations or mislabels (false positives/negatives), degrading training quality.
method: Cognition-aware prompting pipeline with three stages—Comprehension Synthesis (extract themes/intents), Associative Expansion (generate related expressions/entities/intents), and Conclusion (produce final augmented conversation)—which grounds generation to reduce hallucinations and labeling errors; also used to identify necessary turns for accurate dependency-aware alterations.
paper_title: Generalizing Conversational Dense Retrieval via LLM-Cognition Data Augmentation

problem: Uniform sampling of augmented data underfits complex conversations and overfits simple ones, limiting contrastive learning effectiveness.
method: Difficulty-adaptive sample filter that scores conversation complexity (|history turns| + topics × perplexity), selects for each conversation a positive pair with matched diversity (via embedding similarity) and the k hardest negatives, and trains the context encoder with a multi-task objective (ranking loss + contrastive learning with in-batch and selected negatives) to improve robustness and generalization.
paper_title: Generalizing Conversational Dense Retrieval via LLM-Cognition Data Augmentation

problem: Generated explanations in explainable recommenders are often incoherent with the predicted ratings because explanation generators are not explicitly conditioned on the rating.
method: CIER: fine-tune an LLM (LLaMA2-7B with LoRA) to first predict a rating, convert its soft distribution into an embedding via SR2WE (Soft Rating to Word Embedding) mapped to the LLM’s word-embedding space, and then generate a rating-aware explanation conditioned on user/item info and this rating embedding in a pipeline manner to enforce coherence.
paper_title: Coherency Improved Explainable Recommendation via Large Language Model

problem: Evaluating rating–explanation coherence typically requires costly manual annotations or trained classifiers dependent on labeled data.
method: Annotation-free automatic coherence evaluation: prompt GPT-4o to judge sentiment–rating consistency and use a pre-trained BERT sentiment classifier to derive a sentiment score, counting explanations coherent if within ±1 of the rating; leverages public models, improving scalability and reproducibility.
paper_title: Coherency Improved Explainable Recommendation via Large Language Model

problem: Training–inference mismatch (hard labels vs. soft ratings) and weak capture of key aspects reduce explainability and text quality.
method: Targeted training strategies: (i) rating smoothing that redistributes probability mass to neighboring ratings to match soft-rating inference; (ii) curriculum learning that first trains keyword (aspect) generation then transitions to full explanations; and (iii) multi-task learning that jointly optimizes rating prediction and text generation to better capture salient aspects and stabilize generation.
paper_title: Coherency Improved Explainable Recommendation via Large Language Model

problem: Validating dense retrievers requires full-corpus indexing, which is computationally prohibitive; small random subcorpora or RC accuracy do not reliably predict full-scale retrieval performance.
method: Construct a compact hard subcorpus by using a pretrained retriever to gather, for each dev query, the passages of top-k retrieved phrases plus gold passages (H_k). Validate on this index to obtain accuracy that closely tracks full-corpus performance with far fewer vectors, yielding stable model ordering and efficient checkpoint/model selection.
paper_title: Bridging the Training-Inference Gap for Dense Phrase Retrieval

problem: Training objectives for dense phrase retrieval are misaligned with inference, using separate in-passage and in-/pre-batch losses and too few negatives compared to billions at test time.
method: Unify all negatives into a single contrastive loss and weight negatives by source-specific coefficients (lambda) to mimic the abundance of negatives at inference; expand negatives to include all tokens from passages (not just start/end). This better approximates test-time discrimination among massive candidates and improves retrieval accuracy.
paper_title: Bridging the Training-Inference Gap for Dense Phrase Retrieval

problem: Dense phrase retrievers underperform on confusing near-miss candidates due to the lack of hard negatives tailored to phrase retrieval.
method: Mine model-based hard negatives from top-k retrieval results, filter out false negatives by excluding any negatives whose passages contain a gold answer, then fine-tune using all tokens from h hard-negative passages per batch. This exposes the model to challenging, diverse negatives and boosts in-domain phrase/passage retrieval.
paper_title: Bridging the Training-Inference Gap for Dense Phrase Retrieval

problem: Recommendation foundation models like P5 are largely text-only and cannot natively integrate visual item content with personalization across diverse tasks (sequential, direct, explanation).
method: VIP5 introduces multimodal personalized prompts that unify textual, visual, and personalization signals in a shared tokenized space. CLIP-extracted image features are mapped via a lightweight two-layer mapping network into k learnable image tokens and interleaved with text/personalization tokens, augmented by category embeddings, enabling a single encoder–decoder LLM backbone to handle 29 prompts spanning multiple recommendation tasks and zero-shot settings.
paper_title: VIP5: Towards Multimodal Foundation Models for Recommendation

problem: Full fine-tuning of large LLM backbones for recommendation is computationally expensive, memory-intensive, and prone to overfitting, especially when adding visual tokens.
method: Adapter-based parameter-efficient tuning: freeze the P5 backbone and insert bottleneck adapters after self-attention and feed-forward in the encoder, and after cross-attention in the decoder; train only these lightweight adapters and the multimodal mapping layers. This substantially reduces trainable parameters and training cost while improving accuracy across recommendation tasks.
paper_title: VIP5: Towards Multimodal Foundation Models for Recommendation

problem: Heterogeneous treatment effect estimation when treatment is ordinal/continuous and an effect occurs only after a subgroup-specific threshold (trigger) is reached.
method: Introduce trigger-based causal trees that learn, per node/subpopulation, the treatment threshold that maximizes ACE by converting continuous treatment into a binary split at a learned trigger; jointly optimize feature splits and trigger values via a trigger-based partition measure that searches all candidate thresholds.
paper_title: Learning Triggers for Heterogeneous Treatment Effects

problem: Causal trees for HTE often overfit and yield effects that do not generalize to unseen data.
method: Define a generalization-aware splitting criterion that incorporates a validation-set penalty on ACE mismatch (and optionally an honest variance penalty), yielding CT-L, CT-HL, and CT-HV variants that optimize heterogeneity while penalizing non-generalizable splits in a one-stage tree-building process.
paper_title: Learning Triggers for Heterogeneous Treatment Effects

problem: Beyond estimating effects under fixed treatments, practitioners need actionable, individualized treatment prescriptions (e.g., minimum dose or discount) for subgroups.
method: Use the learned per-subgroup triggers from the trigger-based causal trees to prescribe individualized minimum treatment levels that trigger desired outcomes, demonstrating lower ACE estimation error and better heterogeneity discovery than adapted causal trees and propensity-based baselines.
paper_title: Learning Triggers for Heterogeneous Treatment Effects

problem: Autoencoder-based hybrid recommenders predict ratings using MF factors while using neural encodings only as regularizers, leading to hard-to-tune coupling terms, expensive alternating optimization over massive U,V, and constrained dot-product prediction.
method: Neural Representation for Prediction (NRP): remove MF variables and coupling terms, use encoder outputs as the sole user/item representations for prediction, and optimize end-to-end. Provide a constrained objective shown to be equivalent to prior AE+MF as coupling weights → ∞, clarifying that prior neural terms acted merely as regularizers and reducing parameters/training complexity.
paper_title: Neural Representations in Hybrid Recommender Systems: Prediction versus Regularization

problem: Reconstruction decoders and dot-product predictors in hybrid AEs waste memory/time and limit interaction expressiveness.
method: Direct NRP architecture: discard decoders; build user/item embeddings from multiple sources (ratings and side information) via encoders; concatenate to a joint representation and predict with an MLP instead of a dot product, yielding higher accuracy with faster, lower-memory training.
paper_title: Neural Representations in Hybrid Recommender Systems: Prediction versus Regularization

problem: Single-vector node embeddings collapse multiple, potentially unrelated roles/interests of a node (node polysemy), hurting tasks like classification and link prediction.
method: Polysemous DeepWalk: assign K facet-specific target/context embeddings per node; estimate node–facet priors from the adjacency via (symmetric) NMF-based community discovery; in each skip-gram observation, sample an activated facet per node using a heuristic p(v|o) that blends node priors with the observation’s facet mix; optimize a Jensen lower-bound of the likelihood so negative sampling and standard SGD apply with minimal changes.
paper_title: Is a Single Vector Enough? Exploring Node Polysemy for Network Embedding

problem: Polysemy must be supported beyond homogeneous skip-gram models, including heterogeneous graphs and GNNs.
method: Extend to PolyPTE and PolyGCN. PolyPTE: for bipartite graphs, learn facet embeddings for each node type, derive node–facet priors via asymmetric NMF, sample facets per edge, and maximize a lower-bound objective with negative sampling. PolyGCN: instantiate one GCN per facet and build facet-specific neighborhoods by decomposing the interaction matrix A into A_k ∝ P(i,k)·Q(j,k); train each facet GCN unsupervised to produce facet embeddings.
paper_title: Is a Single Vector Enough? Exploring Node Polysemy for Network Embedding

problem: Given multiple facet embeddings per node, how to use them effectively for downstream inference.
method: Classification: concatenate facet embeddings, optionally weighted by facet probabilities. Link prediction: compute node–node similarity as a probability-weighted sum over all facet-pair similarities, aggregating evidence from multiple roles.
paper_title: Is a Single Vector Enough? Exploring Node Polysemy for Network Embedding

problem: Link recommendation trained/evaluated on observed graphs confounds relevance with exposure: many missing links are false negatives due to users not being exposed, yielding biased risk estimates and poor generalization.
method: Define true risk as performance if all users were exposed to all items, and propose three propensity-aware estimators for it: (i) inverse-propensity-weighted risk (Rw) that upweights positives and downweights negatives using exposure probabilities and predicted link probs; (ii) a PU-style estimator (RPU) that removes an appropriate mass of negatives per positive; and (iii) an 'add-positives' estimator (RAP). Provide bias/variance analyses with sufficient conditions showing lower bias than naive evaluation and a variance ordering Var(RAP) < Var(Rw) < Var(RPU) < Var(naive).
paper_title: Correcting Exposure Bias for Link Recommendation

problem: Propensity scores are unknown in practice, and naive maximum likelihood on observed links does not target true risk and admits trivial solutions.
method: Derive a Rademacher-style generalization bound showing that minimizing the proposed risk estimators controls true risk, and introduce a joint learning objective that combines the estimator (Rw/RPU/RAP) with a negative log-likelihood term to simultaneously learn propensities and link probabilities from implicit data while preventing collapse; trainable with gradient methods and flexible parametric models.
paper_title: Correcting Exposure Bias for Link Recommendation

problem: Iteratively training recommenders on their own logged data induces feedback loops that increasingly suppress low-propensity yet relevant items.
method: Formulate a category-based stochastic model of iterative recommendation and prove that naive training skews recommendations toward high-propensity categories at a rate governed by qv/qw; show theoretically and via simulations on MAG-based data that propensity-corrected training mitigates this amplification and improves diversity (e.g., higher field-of-study entropy) while improving true-risk metrics on semi-synthetic ground truth.
paper_title: Correcting Exposure Bias for Link Recommendation

problem: Generic MLM/Seq2Seq pre-training under-develops sentence-level representations needed for dense retrieval, and prior auto-encoding objectives let the decoder rely too much on its own input/context.
method: A novel masked auto-encoder workflow with dual masks: the encoder sees a moderately masked sentence to produce a sentence embedding, and the decoder sees a differently and aggressively masked sentence and reconstructs the original via MLM conditioned on the sentence embedding—forcing the encoder to capture rich semantics for retrieval.
paper_title: RetroMAE: Pre-Training Retrieval-oriented Language Models Via Masked Auto-Encoder

problem: Reconstruction must be demanding enough to depend on the encoder’s representation while keeping pre-training efficient.
method: Asymmetric architecture and masking: a full BERT-like encoder plus a single-layer transformer decoder; moderate encoder masking (15–30%) and aggressive decoder masking (50–70%). This weakens decoder capacity and context, compelling reliance on the sentence embedding without increasing compute over BERT-style pre-training.
paper_title: RetroMAE: Pre-Training Retrieval-oriented Language Models Via Masked Auto-Encoder

problem: Conventional MLM/AE use only masked tokens for loss and a single shared context per prediction, yielding limited and homogeneous training signals.
method: Enhanced decoding with two-stream self-attention and position-specific attention masks in a one-layer decoder, enabling reconstruction of 100% tokens with token-specific visible contexts while conditioning on the sentence embedding—substantially improving data efficiency and supervision diversity for retrieval-oriented embeddings.
paper_title: RetroMAE: Pre-Training Retrieval-oriented Language Models Via Masked Auto-Encoder

problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, and do prior claims that fine-tuning decentralizes pre-trained knowledge generalize beyond BERT+DPR?
method: Reproduce and extend prior analysis via layer-wise linear probing and integrated-gradient neuron attribution on two datasets (NQ, MS MARCO) and three model families (DPR/BERT-CLS, Contriever/BERT-Mean, RepLlama/Llama-EOS). Confirm for DPR that pre-trained BERT already encodes most discriminative capacity and fine-tuning mainly shifts neuron activations, but show this behavior does not universally hold for mean-pooled (Contriever) and decoder-based (RepLlama) retrievers.
paper_title: Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition

problem: How do different embedding strategies (CLS vs mean pooling vs EOS) affect where discriminative retrieval signals reside and how fine-tuning redistributes knowledge across layers?
method: Compare pooling strategies using layer-wise probes with varying candidate set sizes (N=2–5) and IG-based activation maps. Show that mean pooling narrows the base vs fine-tuned gap and reduces intermediate-layer specialization (fewer activated neurons), while EOS pooling in RepLlama yields strong late-layer gains and fewer intermediate activations, indicating architecture/pooling-dependent adaptation.
paper_title: Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition

problem: Reproducibility gaps in probing/attribution for dense retrieval (unclear negatives, probe design, and reporting) impede verification of knowledge acquisition claims.
method: Provide a fully specified, reproducible pipeline: standardized linear classifier over concatenated query+passage embeddings, explicit hard-negative construction for NQ/MS MARCO, fixed hyperparameters, and an attribution protocol with per-example normalization and threshold-based percentage reporting; release code and processed datasets.
paper_title: Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition

problem: Dense retrievers trained with random negatives get uninformative signals, while top-k hard negatives often include false negatives, causing unstable optimization (low gradient means or high gradient variance).
method: Empirically analyze gradient statistics vs. negative rank and find that negatives ranked near positives have high gradient means and low variances. Use relevance-score proximity to positives as a cheap proxy to target these ‘ambiguous’ negatives that are informative yet less likely to be false.
paper_title: SimANS: Simple Ambiguous Negatives Sampling for Dense Text Retrieval

problem: Need a scalable, plug-and-play sampling scheme that prioritizes ambiguous negatives without computing gradients and avoids both too-easy and too-hard items.
method: SimANS defines a sampling probability over candidate negatives using an exponential function of the score difference between a negative s(q,d) and a positive s(q,d+), with two hyperparameters (a for density, b for peak). It restricts candidates to ANN-retrieved top-k, replaces the positive mean with a randomly sampled positive for efficiency, precomputes probabilities, and samples per batch (O(k) cost), making it easy to integrate with dual-encoder retrievers and compatible with KD/adversarial training.
paper_title: SimANS: Simple Ambiguous Negatives Sampling for Dense Text Retrieval

problem: Demonstrate generality and practical gains across models and datasets, including industrial settings, with minimal overhead.
method: Apply SimANS to AR2, ANCE, and RocketQA and to a Bing multilingual dataset, achieving consistent MRR/Recall improvements and slight latency increases; ablations (Doc-Sim, Nearest-K) confirm the superiority of score-difference-based stochastic sampling over document-similarity or deterministic nearest-k selection.
paper_title: SimANS: Simple Ambiguous Negatives Sampling for Dense Text Retrieval

problem: Conversational dense retrieval often concatenates full conversation history, which is long and noisy, causing shortcut history dependency and poor focus on the current turn.
method: Context-denoised query reformulation via pseudo relevance judgments (PRJ) that assess each historical turn using both its query and associated ground-truth passage by measuring the actual retrieval impact when concatenated to the current query; only PRJ-relevant turns are kept for reformulation.
paper_title: History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval

problem: Single gold passage per turn provides weak supervision, and noisy history can mislead contrastive training.
method: History-aware contrastive learning that mines additional supervision from history using PRJ: use PRJ-relevant historical gold passages as pseudo positives and PRJ-irrelevant ones as historical hard negatives, combined with standard in-batch/retrieved negatives in a dual-encoder (ANCE) framework.
paper_title: History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval

problem: Historical ground-truth passages may be unavailable in real deployments.
method: A practical adaptation that substitutes historical gold passages with top-k retrieved passages to perform PRJ and supervision mining, preserving gains and guiding k to control noise.
paper_title: History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval

problem: Joint embeddings often underuse cross-modal correspondence and degrade with missing/noisy modalities, limiting cross-modality and shared-representation learning.
method: A composite objective that maximizes correlation between modality-specific latent projections (mini-batch correlation loss) and includes fused-, self-, and cross-reconstruction losses, enforcing information sharing and robustness to absent/noisy inputs.
paper_title: Deep Multimodal Representation Learning from Temporal Data

problem: Modality reliability varies over time (noise, occlusion), so static fusion weights hurt robustness.
method: A dynamic weighting (soft attention) module that computes bilinear coherence between each modality’s current input and the previous fused state, normalizes to weights, and adaptively gates inputs to the GRU at every timestep.
paper_title: Deep Multimodal Representation Learning from Temporal Data

problem: Robotic ASR often misrecognizes entity/attribute words in instructions due to generic ASR training, OOV terms, noise, and dynamically changing environments; static or naive multimodal approaches either require paired inputs at all times or degrade under irrelevant context.
method: Treat the robot’s visual scene as dynamic prior knowledge: use dense image captioning on the egocentric view, then a lightweight BiLSTM sequence labeler (BIO tagging over caption tokens) to select salient bias phrases and build a dynamic prefix trie per scene. This compact, on-the-fly bias vocabulary conditions ASR decoding without requiring persistent multimodal coupling or large static lexicons.
paper_title: Can Visual Context Improve Automatic Speech Recognition for an Embodied Agent?

problem: Conventional shallow-fusion biasing at word level fails due to early pruning of partial subword matches; subword weight-pushing can over-bias, degrade on general speech, and needs costly WFST updates for changing vocabularies.
method: A new shallow-fusion decoder with word-level biasing enabled by subword look-ahead: (1) cumulative-probability sampling to curb low-probability expansions and over-biasing, (2) contextual rescoring that interpolates CTC with an n-gram LM and applies unigram-based boosts plus a conditional lexicon constraint (penalize non-bias OOVs, boost OOVs in bias list), and (3) bias-aware non-greedy pruning using a rescoring-likelihood over the bias trie to retain candidates likely to complete bias words. This avoids WFST recompilation, preserves intrinsic ranking for non-biased beams, and yields large WER reductions (≈59% relative).
paper_title: Can Visual Context Improve Automatic Speech Recognition for an Embodied Agent?

problem: Low-resource ASR needs phonetic, transferable speech representations; supervised transfer ignores abundant unlabeled target audio, while self-supervised contrastive models may learn less phonetic, harder-to-transfer features.
method: Unified multitask pretraining that combines supervised phoneme-level CTC with phonetically-aware contrastive learning on a wav2vec 2.0-style encoder (conv feature extractor + Transformer + vector quantizer). Contrastive loss is applied on both labeled high-resource and unlabeled target-domain/language audio to align contextual and quantized latents, yielding representations that generalize across languages and domains.
paper_title: UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data

problem: Quantized contrastive models (e.g., wav2vec 2.0) suffer from codebook collapse and weak alignment of discrete latents to phonetic units, limiting ASR transfer.
method: Phoneme-aligned quantizer training via mixed-representation CTC: during CTC on labeled data, randomly replace a proportion of contextual embeddings with their time-aligned quantized latents, forcing codewords to predict phonemes and unifying supervised and self-supervised spaces; coupled with a codebook diversity loss to activate more entries and produce a phonetic-aware, non-collapsed codebook.
paper_title: UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data

problem: Diffusion-based text-to-audio models require many iterative steps, and one-step consistency methods underperform due to curved ODE trajectories, limiting real-time deployment.
method: Introduce FlashAudio using rectified flows for text-conditioned generation, applying a single reflow step to learn near-straight transport paths; initialize from a strong pre-trained conditional flow-matching model and distill the straightened ODE into a one-step Euler update for ultra-fast sampling with high fidelity.
paper_title: FlashAudio: Rectified Flows for Fast and High-fidelity Text-to-Audio Generation

problem: Uniform timestep sampling and unconstrained noise–data pairing in rectified flow training waste capacity and destabilize learning.
method: Bifocal Samplers reweight timesteps—logit-normal to emphasize challenging mid-range times for 1-RF, and a mixture-of-exponentials to focus on boundaries during reflow (2-RF); Immiscible Flow assigns batch-wise noise–data pairs via Hungarian matching to minimize total distance while preserving Gaussian noise, improving efficiency and stability.
paper_title: FlashAudio: Rectified Flows for Fast and High-fidelity Text-to-Audio Generation

problem: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) amplifies accumulation errors and perturbs marginal distributions in rectified flows, degrading quality at higher guidance scales.
method: Anchored Optimization: use the ω=1 trajectory as a reference and optimize only the unconditional embedding during guided sampling to refine the effective guidance scale, yielding better audio quality and text–audio alignment without destabilizing the flow.
paper_title: FlashAudio: Rectified Flows for Fast and High-fidelity Text-to-Audio Generation

problem: ASR post-correction requires accurately identifying which tokens are wrong; implicit detection lacks explicit supervision and explicit edit-type detection is brittle, while standard LMs are either unidirectional or require N-pass and tend to learn trivial copy at low WER.
method: Introduce a soft error detector: a Transformer encoder trained with an anti-copy language modeling loss that adds a special GT token and a regularization term to discourage trivial copy. This yields calibrated, single-pass, bidirectional per-token probabilities that serve as soft correctness scores for detection.
paper_title: SoftCorrect: Error Correction with Soft Detection for Automatic Speech Recognition

problem: CTC-based non-AR correction typically duplicates all tokens and allows free alignment, which can change correct tokens and increases latency; we need correction to focus only on detected errors.
method: Design a constrained CTC loss in the decoder that duplicates only tokens flagged as errors and fixes undetected tokens as alignment anchors. This focuses edits on error spans, preserves parallel decoding, reduces latency, and includes robustness via randomly treating a small fraction of correct tokens as pseudo-errors during training.
paper_title: SoftCorrect: Error Correction with Soft Detection for Automatic Speech Recognition

problem: A single ASR hypothesis hides uncertainty, limiting error detection accuracy.
method: Leverage ASR beam n-best: align candidates, encode them jointly, perform position-wise selection of the highest-probability token to form a better candidate, and compute a soft error score by combining encoder LM probabilities with ASR acoustic probabilities to threshold incorrect tokens.
paper_title: SoftCorrect: Error Correction with Soft Detection for Automatic Speech Recognition

problem: Training a single LM for heterogeneous ASR applications (VA vs. STT) leads to accuracy loss due to distributional mismatch and data imbalance when naïvely merging corpora.
method: A balancing scheme that builds a VA/STT-balanced dev set, derives redistribution probability masses from optimal interpolation weights of application-specific LMs, and rescales per-source count-merging sampling weights accordingly to produce balanced sampling probabilities for AA NNLM training.
paper_title: Application-Agnostic Language Modeling for On-Device ASR

problem: A single AA LM underperforms app-specific LMs, but on-device constraints prohibit large ensembles or high-latency models.
method: Mixture FOFE NNLM: multiple parallel FOFE feed-forward sub-networks with a learned mixture sub-network that outputs normalized expert weights; their weighted outputs are averaged before the softmax. FOFE n-gram contexts preserve low latency under short context limits, improving WER over baseline AA FOFE and Transformers with moderate size increase.
paper_title: Application-Agnostic Language Modeling for On-Device ASR

problem: Need a single on-device LM that preserves app-specific accuracy and speed without mixture overhead or doubling disk size.
method: Application-Dependent FOFE NNLM: one model with separate FOFE sub-networks and softmax biases per application; during training, route data and backprop only through the corresponding sub-network; at inference, select the sub-network via invocation signal (VA vs. STT). Shares embeddings to control size, matching/outperforming app-specific WER while maintaining or improving latency and halving disk footprint vs. two separate models.
paper_title: Application-Agnostic Language Modeling for On-Device ASR

problem: ASR-only encoder pre-training for end-to-end speech translation fails to imbue the encoder with higher-level linguistic understanding and cross-lingual mapping, overburdening the encoder-decoder during fine-tuning.
method: A curriculum pre-training scheme with increasing task difficulty: (1) ASR pre-training for transcription, (2) frame-based masked language modeling for sentence understanding, and (3) frame-based bilingual lexicon translation for cross-lingual word mapping; applied hierarchically across encoder layers (bottom N layers for ASR/FMLM, top layers for FBLT) before ST fine-tuning.
paper_title: Curriculum Pre-training for End-to-End Speech Translation

problem: The encoder lacks semantic understanding beyond acoustic-to-token alignment when trained only on ASR.
method: Frame-based Masked Language Model (FMLM): use forced alignment to locate word-level speech segments, mask 15% segments (fill with utterance mean), average corresponding encoder states, and predict the source BPE token distribution via KL loss computed at an intermediate encoder layer, teaching the encoder to infer masked content and sentence meaning.
paper_title: Curriculum Pre-training for End-to-End Speech Translation

problem: End-to-end ST encoders have weak explicit cross-lingual alignment, forcing the decoder to learn mappings from scratch.
method: Frame-based Bilingual Lexicon Translation (FBLT): for each word-aligned speech segment, predict the target-language word distribution (for all segments, masked or not) using KL loss at the top encoder layer; obtain alignments via GIZA++/grow-diag-final on ST triples, and supervise ASR-only data using a bilingual lexicon built from parallel text, injecting strong cross-lingual signals and reducing decoder burden.
paper_title: Curriculum Pre-training for End-to-End Speech Translation

problem: Text-guided diffusion models struggle to precisely edit target audio events while preserving unrelated content, often requiring retraining or manual masks.
method: PPAE, a training-free plug-in for audio latent diffusion models, performs token-specific cross-attention map manipulation during denoising. It inverts the source audio into the model’s latent/noise space and injects edited-prompt cross-attention to localize changes while maintaining the original structure.
paper_title: Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing with Diffusion Models

problem: Direct attention-map replacement in audio leads to abrupt transitions and degraded quality during editing.
method: A local Fuser module that progressively blends source and target cross-attention maps within each diffusion step using a cosine-annealed schedule and token-level controls (replace/refine/reweight), yielding smooth, precise local edits without masks.
paper_title: Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing with Diffusion Models

problem: Classifier-free guidance scale selection is highly sample- and task-dependent in audio, making precise edits brittle.
method: Guidance bootstrapping across multiple scales with CLAP-based filtering to automatically select the best-aligned edit, improving robustness without manual tuning.
paper_title: Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing with Diffusion Models

problem: Coordinating non-prehensile pushing with prehensile grasping in clutter without hand-designed heuristics, so pushes set up future grasps and grasps leverage past pushes.
method: Joint, model-free deep Q-learning with two fully convolutional networks that map RGB-D orthographic heightmaps (rotated into 16 orientations) to dense pixel-wise Q-values for push and grasp motion primitives; greedy action selection; end-to-end self-supervision where grasp success provides the main reward so pushes learn value via enabling future grasps (optionally aided by change-detection intrinsic rewards).
paper_title: Learning Synergies between Pushing and Grasping with Self-supervised Deep Reinforcement Learning

problem: Visuomotor RL on real robots is sample-inefficient due to large state–action spaces and costly rollouts.
method: Pixel-wise parameterization of action primitives (10 cm pushes and top-down parallel-jaw grasps) and fully convolutional action-value functions to share features across locations/orientations and evaluate ~1.6M actions per forward pass; prioritized replay and epsilon-greedy exploration enable self-supervised training on a single robot in a few hours with generalization to novel objects.
paper_title: Learning Synergies between Pushing and Grasping with Self-supervised Deep Reinforcement Learning

problem: End-to-end visuomotor policies require large task-specific real data and struggle to generalize across tasks/robots.
method: A modular architecture with low-dimensional latent interfaces: (i) a variational affordance encoder–decoder (RGB→pixel-level affordances) whose latent vector encodes scene affordances, (ii) a supervised policy that maps the affordance latent plus camera pose to an action latent, and (iii) a trajectory VAE that decodes the action latent to joint-space trajectories. β-VAE bottlenecks ensure smooth, compact spaces enabling data-efficient training and composability for transfer.
paper_title: Affordance Learning for End-to-End Visuomotor Robot Control

problem: Collecting pixel-level affordance annotations is costly and limits scalability.
method: An automatic Blender-based dataset generator with extensive domain randomization (object shapes, textures, clutter, lighting, camera) to render paired RGB images and affordance masks at scale—no manual labeling. Trains the VAED on RGB-only inputs and achieves competitive UMD benchmark performance, demonstrating scalability and generalization.
paper_title: Affordance Learning for End-to-End Visuomotor Robot Control

problem: Achieving robust sim-to-real visuomotor control from RGB under clutter without depth sensing.
method: Train perception on domain-randomized synthetic RGB; train the policy in simulation with a differentiable forward-kinematics supervision signal to map affordance latents to action latents decoded by the trajectory VAE. Deployed zero-shot on a Franka Panda for ball-into-container, showing high success and robustness to heavy clutter via affordance-centric latent representations.
paper_title: Affordance Learning for End-to-End Visuomotor Robot Control

problem: Learning action-conditioned visual dynamics when most available videos lack action labels, making direct supervised training impossible.
method: Introduce a latent action variable z that explains frame-to-frame transitions; treat actions as observed for interaction data and unobserved for observation-only data. Train a probabilistic model with amortized variational inference using two encoders q_act(z|a) and q_inv(z|x_t, x_{t+1}) aligned via a Jensen–Shannon divergence, a transition model p(x_{t+1}|x_{1:t}, z_{1:t}), and an action decoder p(a_t|z_t).
paper_title: Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction

problem: Bridging embodiment/domain shift (e.g., human vs. robot) so that observational videos can inform robot dynamics without collapsing domain-specific nuances.
method: Partition the latent action z into shared (z_shared) and domain-specific (z_domain) components with domain-dependent priors: a shared prior for z_shared and separate priors for each domain’s z_domain. For humans, use a temporally correlated LSTM prior conditioned on the initial observation, preserving embodiment-specific structure while sharing transferable action semantics.
paper_title: Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction

problem: Enabling robots to perform tool-use tasks without costly expert robot demonstrations, leveraging only random robot interactions plus human videos.
method: Use the learned POI dynamics with sampling-based visual MPC (CEM) to plan action sequences in pixel space. The improved action-conditioned predictions from mixed observation–interaction training translate into higher task success on multi-object tool-use, approaching an oracle trained with kinesthetic demos.
paper_title: Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction

problem: Robot manipulation policies trained from pixels struggle to generalize across unseen object instances or domains, while off-the-shelf detectors are constrained by their label sets and domain shift.
method: Introduce a two-level, object-centric perception stack: (1) a task-agnostic meta-attention using an RPN pretrained on generic datasets to propose object hypotheses with bounding boxes, and (2) a task-specific attention learned from a few trajectories that selects relevant objects by applying Boltzmann-weighted soft attention over pretrained CNN (AlexNet conv5) features with entropy regularization; the attention outputs object locations for downstream control.
paper_title: Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning

problem: Learning reliable visuomotor policies with few samples that remain robust to clutter and new object instances.
method: Feed attended object coordinates, along with robot state, into a policy learned via guided policy search; freeze the attention during RL to stabilize learning. This object-centric observation enables policies that outperform end-to-end pixel-based baselines in generalization and robustness, and supports multi-object tasks by stacking multiple attention vectors.
paper_title: Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning

problem: Practitioners need to control whether perception generalizes broadly (category-level) or narrowly (instance-specific) without collecting large labeled detection datasets.
method: Enable user-tunable generalization by few-shot finetuning of the task-specific attention with curated demonstrations: add distractors to narrow the scope (e.g., prefer a specific mug) or include diverse instances to broaden it (e.g., generalize from oranges to citrus), all without retraining the meta-attention.
paper_title: Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning

problem: Contact-rich robotic manipulation policies are hard to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and perception/control noise; how can a robot autonomously make learning faster and more robust by leveraging environmental constraints?
method: A two-loop framework that lets the robot actively place physical fixtures to scaffold learning. The outer loop selects a fixture pose from a depth image to maximize the inner loop’s learning progress; the inner loop learns a vision-based manipulation policy via RL (A3C with operational space control). A CNN-based Q-function over (image, fixture pose) plus CEM is used for pose selection, yielding large speedups in peg insertion, wrench use, and shallow-depth insertion.
paper_title: Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills

problem: Optimizing fixture placement is a contextual bandit with continuous actions and discontinuous rewards (small pose changes can flip success to failure), where standard Lipschitz/zooming methods fail.
method: Smoothed Zooming algorithm: adaptively covers the continuous action space with shrinking balls; uses UCB1 to pick a ball and uniformly samples within it to smooth discontinuities, updating averages and radii while activating new balls as areas are exposed. This improves sample efficiency for high-dimensional contextual inputs (images) and discontinuous rewards during outer-loop training.
paper_title: Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills

problem: Skills learned with hard physical fixtures may fail once fixtures are removed; how to retain performance when removing scaffolds.
method: Replace hard constraints with a virtual potential field that mirrors the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move/remove the physical fixture while continuing RL fine-tuning from the fixture-trained policy. This maintains task success as scaffolds are phased out.
paper_title: Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills

problem: Robots fail to detect reliable 6-DoF grasps on transparent objects in clutter because RGB-D depth is invalid and single-view cues are ambiguous.
method: Build a multi-view Depth Likelihood Volume (DLV) from light field (plenoptic) observations, generalizing prior single-view DLV to a 3D workspace via multi-ray triangulation across sub-aperture images and multiple camera viewpoints to estimate transparency occupancy per 3D point and evaluate grasp candidates within this volume.
paper_title: GlassLoc: Plenoptic Grasp Pose Detection in Transparent Clutter

problem: Specular highlights on transparent surfaces induce spurious high-likelihood "virtual" surfaces that corrupt grasp reasoning.
method: Apply reflection suppression during DLV fusion by computing variance of ray differences across viewpoints for saturated/high-likelihood rays and rejecting rays that exhibit high variance and are not maximal along shallower depths, thereby removing specular artifacts.
paper_title: GlassLoc: Plenoptic Grasp Pose Detection in Transparent Clutter

problem: Lack of reliable labels/features for grasp classification on transparent objects due to faulty depth sensing hinders learning robust 6-DoF grasps.
method: Represent each grasp as a 3D cuboid in the DLV, voxelize and derive nine 2D slice/average/differential feature maps along gripper axes, train a CNN (LeNet) using data generated by temporarily wrapping objects to obtain point clouds and mapping positive/negative grasp candidates into the DLV, and use a particle-filter-based search in SE(3) to select high-confidence grasps.
paper_title: GlassLoc: Plenoptic Grasp Pose Detection in Transparent Clutter

problem: Robots need object physical properties (mass, center of mass, friction, inertia) to perform dynamic in-hand manipulation, but explicit measurement/supervision and structured environments are impractical.
method: Self-supervised multi-action tactile exploration with a GelSight sensor: two exploratory procedures—tilting (20°/45°) and shaking with a loosened grip—are encoded by CNNs (spatial marker maps) and an LSTM (temporal sequence) and fused into a low-dimensional physical feature embedding. Trained end-to-end using only final swing-up angle as supervision, the embedding captures object and sensor dynamics and can be disentangled to regress mass/CoM/inertia and classify friction.
paper_title: SwingBot: Learning Physical Features from In-hand Tactile Exploration for Dynamic Swing-up Manipulation

problem: Selecting control parameters to achieve accurate swing-up on previously unseen objects with varying dynamics, without hand-tuned physics or online system ID.
method: A forward dynamics predictor maps (learned tactile embedding, candidate gripper-loosening width) to the final swing angle; at inference the robot samples actions and chooses the one whose predicted angle matches the goal. This task-oriented embedding enables open-loop control that outperforms no-exploration and single-action baselines and generalizes to unseen objects (≈17.2° mean error).
paper_title: SwingBot: Learning Physical Features from In-hand Tactile Exploration for Dynamic Swing-up Manipulation

problem: Language-guided manipulation models overfit and fail to generalize spatial relations when object locations are encoded as coordinate lists processed by fully connected layers.
method: Encode detected objects as a quantized 2D binary grid with per-class channels plus a size map, and use a language-conditioned convolutional U-Net to predict start/end heatmaps with spatial-softmax to extract coordinates. The convolutional, translation-equivariant bias enables robust spatial reasoning (e.g., rows, relative offsets) and outperforms list+FC and end-to-end baselines.
paper_title: Spatial Reasoning from Natural Language Instructions for Robot Manipulation

problem: Dataset biases and ambiguous targets cause language-only or unguided models to attend to spurious correlations (e.g., block IDs implying offsets), degrading end-location prediction.
method: Introduce multi-head attention over BiLSTM-encoded instructions to produce four embeddings: two that ‘soft-select’ scene objects via per-pixel correlation with the object grid, and two that encode spatial relations. This grounded attention focuses on relevant tokens (offsets/targets), mitigating bias and improving generalization.
paper_title: Spatial Reasoning from Natural Language Instructions for Robot Manipulation

problem: Executing natural-language pick-and-place in real settings with limited training data requires reliable perception-grounded grounding rather than fully end-to-end learning.
method: Adopt a pipelined system: fine-tuned SSD object detector for localization, followed by the proposed Lang-UNet to regress start/end coordinates. Deployed on a Dobot arm for card pick-and-place with high success, validating practicality and data efficiency over end-to-end approaches.
paper_title: Spatial Reasoning from Natural Language Instructions for Robot Manipulation

problem: Visual robotic insertion policies trained offline often fail to generalize zero-shot to novel connectors, and ground-truth rewards/localization are unavailable during deployment for online adaptation.
method: LOTJ: a self-rewarding offline-to-online finetuning pipeline that pretrains policy/value and a visual reward classifier with Implicit Q-Learning on diverse offline data, then finetunes on the new connector using the learned reward predictions as supervision with balanced offline/online replay and stabilized training (e.g., frozen conv layers, deterministic encoding), enabling rapid adaptation without external rewards or precise localization.
paper_title: Learning on the Job: Self-Rewarding Offline-to-Online Finetuning for Industrial Insertion of Novel Connectors from Vision

problem: Domain shift across connectors makes both policies and reward classifiers brittle; enforcing full domain invariance discards connector-specific cues needed for success, while unconstrained features overfit to domains.
method: Domain Adversarial Information Bottleneck (DAIB): a split latent z=(z_I,z_S) where z_I is trained to be domain-invariant via an adversarial domain classifier and z_S is constrained by a variational information bottleneck to carry limited domain-specific information; applied to both reward and policy encoders within IQL to balance invariance and specificity, improving cross-connector generalization and enabling effective finetuning.
paper_title: Learning on the Job: Self-Rewarding Offline-to-Online Finetuning for Industrial Insertion of Novel Connectors from Vision

problem: Existing insertion systems rely on precise localization or are tailored to single connectors, limiting robustness in unstructured settings with large pose uncertainty.
method: A vision-only real-world system trained on a large offline dataset (≈70k trajectories over 50 connectors) with DAIB-enabled reward and IQL policies, then finetuned online on unseen connectors; demonstrates >75% success after ~200 trials under ±10–20 mm initial pose noise and outperforms localization+handcrafted control and prior RL baselines.
paper_title: Learning on the Job: Self-Rewarding Offline-to-Online Finetuning for Industrial Insertion of Novel Connectors from Vision

problem: Robots must both explore unseen environments and navigate to visually specified goals, but prior systems use separate models or subgoal generators, adding complexity and reducing generalization.
method: A single Transformer-based policy with attention-based goal masking that optionally includes/excludes the goal image token; trained with a Bernoulli mask (p=0.5) so one network jointly learns task-agnostic exploration and goal-directed navigation.
paper_title: NoMaD: Goal Masked Diffusion Policies for Navigation and Exploration

problem: Modeling safe, multimodal action sequences from images—especially without a goal—is hard for point-estimate or simple autoregressive policies.
method: A goal-conditioned action diffusion policy that models a conditional distribution over future action sequences given the observation context (and optional goal) using a 1D conditional U-Net; enables expressive, multimodal, collision-averse action sampling and is the first successful goal-conditioned action diffusion model deployed on a real robot.
paper_title: NoMaD: Goal Masked Diffusion Policies for Navigation and Exploration

problem: Short-horizon policies struggle with long-horizon search and navigation in large, novel environments.
method: Integrate the unified diffusion policy with a topological episodic memory and a frontier-based planner (ViKiNG-style) that builds a graph from observations and uses the policy’s distance predictions to plan subgoals for long-horizon exploration and goal-seeking.
paper_title: NoMaD: Goal Masked Diffusion Policies for Navigation and Exploration

problem: Pre-trained generalist robot policies are largely vision-centric and, when finetuned on small datasets with new sensors (touch, audio), tend to ignore these modalities, limiting performance in partially observable settings.
method: FuSe: a finetuning recipe that grounds all sensor modalities in natural language by adding two auxiliary losses to behavior cloning—(i) a CLIP-style multimodal contrastive loss that aligns observation embeddings (built by re-encoding modality tokens and fusing with attention) with language instructions across all modality combinations, and (ii) a multimodal language-generative loss that predicts the instruction from observations using a shared transformer head with modality tokens—thereby linking heterogeneous sensors to the policy’s semantic space and preventing over-reliance on vision.
paper_title: Beyond Sight: Finetuning Generalist Robot Policies with Heterogeneous Sensors via Language Grounding

problem: There is a scarcity of physically grounded datasets that jointly couple vision, touch, audio, language, and robot actions, hindering learning of multimodal manipulation policies.
method: Collect and release 26.9K real-robot teleoperation trajectories spanning three tasks with synchronized RGB (3rd/wrist), DIGIT tactile, audio, proprio/IMU, actions, and language; augment instructions via LLM-based rephrasing to expand open-vocabulary supervision for modality-specific attributes (e.g., feel/sound), enabling effective multimodal grounding with limited data.
paper_title: Beyond Sight: Finetuning Generalist Robot Policies with Heterogeneous Sensors via Language Grounding

problem: Robotic policies need zero-shot multimodal prompting and compositional cross-modal reasoning (e.g., select by feel/sound or chain perception-to-language-to-action), and methods should transfer across different policy backbones.
method: Apply FuSe to distinct generalist architectures—Octo and a 3B PaliGemma-based VLA—integrating heterogeneous sensors into a transformer policy; leverage the generative head for cross-modal chaining (e.g., infer button sound label from audio then act). This yields >20% success improvements over baselines and unlocks capabilities like multimodal and compositional prompting and on-the-fly object descriptions.
paper_title: Beyond Sight: Finetuning Generalist Robot Policies with Heterogeneous Sensors via Language Grounding

problem: Robots lack a general, scalable way to infer what configurations of a scene are plausibly reachable via interaction (visual affordances), especially for unseen objects and environments without action annotations or in-domain data collection.
method: Learn visual affordances as a conditional distribution over future images p(og|oc) using a two-stage generative model: (1) a VQ-VAE to discretize images into latent codes, and (2) an autoregressive Transformer that predicts goal latents conditioned on the current-scene latents. Trained on large-scale passive internet video frame pairs, this latent-space conditional generation yields diverse, plausible, and compositionally generalizable goal images without action labels or lab fine-tuning.
paper_title: Visual Affordance Prediction for Guiding Robot Exploration

problem: Exploration for goal-conditioned manipulation policies often relies on undirected heuristics or goal samplers that produce implausible or non-diverse goals, limiting emergent behaviors and generalization.
method: Use the learned affordance model as a goal-sampling distribution to drive self-supervised exploration: sample a goal image given the current scene, execute rollouts, and train a goal-conditioned policy via behavior cloning with hindsight relabeling (set each trajectory’s goal to its final frame). This affordance-driven exploration yields broader interactions and higher success on real-robot tasks (pushing, pick-and-place, stacking) than curiosity, CVAE, and Pix2Pix baselines.
paper_title: Visual Affordance Prediction for Guiding Robot Exploration

problem: Robot caregiving lacks a large-scale, diverse, expert-driven dataset that captures real-world ADLs with the multimodal signals needed for perception under occlusion, safe physical contact, and long-horizon task structure.
method: OpenRoboCare: 19.8 hours over 315 sessions from 21 occupational therapists performing 15 ADL tasks on two manikins, with five synchronized modalities (RGB-D, pose tracking, eye-gaze, whole-body tactile sensing, and action/task annotations) collected via an OT-in-the-loop, clinically grounded protocol and released with documentation.
paper_title: OpenRoboCare : A Multimodal Multi-Task Expert Demonstration Dataset for Robot Caregiving

problem: Safe assistive manipulation requires ground-truth contact locations and force distributions on the care recipient, which prior datasets lack or only measure on the robot side.
method: A calibrated, flexible, fabric-based whole-body piezo-resistive tactile skin mounted on manikins (88 sensors total; 44 per manikin) covering arms, legs, and torso, with 0.05–3 N/cm² sensitivity and low hysteresis, synchronized with other streams to provide dense force/contact maps across caregiving tasks.
paper_title: OpenRoboCare : A Multimodal Multi-Task Expert Demonstration Dataset for Robot Caregiving

problem: State-of-the-art pose and long-horizon activity recognition models underperform in caregiving due to heavy occlusions, multi-body interactions, and domain shift, and there is no benchmark or scalable labeling pipeline.
method: A benchmarking suite plus a semi-automatic pose annotation pipeline that combines limited manual multi-view 2D labels with a fine-tuned YOLOv11 detector and triangulation to recover 3D keypoints; evaluations show large performance gaps but substantial gains from small-sample fine-tuning, and the fine-tuned pose model is released as a resource.
paper_title: OpenRoboCare : A Multimodal Multi-Task Expert Demonstration Dataset for Robot Caregiving

problem: Scalable, robust, and accurate simulation of soft grippers and deformable objects for grasping is lacking; existing engines allow penetrations/inversions or IPC is too slow for large-scale data generation.
method: A GPU-parallel, multi-environment IPC-based simulator with environment isolation for CCD and step-size filtering, per-environment stopping criteria and failure isolation, and parallel linear solves—guaranteeing intersection- and inversion-free, high-fidelity frictional contact while achieving up to 16–48× speedups.
paper_title: GRIP: A General Robotic Incremental Potential Contact Simulation Dataset for Unified Deformable-Rigid Coupled Grasping

problem: No large-scale, physically valid grasp dataset that includes soft grippers, deformable manipulands, and uni-/bimanual settings to train and evaluate grasping models.
method: A fully automated pipeline that (i) prepares simulation-ready assets (tetrahedralization, material/friction randomization), (ii) synthesizes candidates via GPD for UMI and an adapted DexGraspNet for LEAP Hand with a normal-alignment energy and IK-based de-penetration to satisfy IPC’s intersection-free initialization, (iii) composes bimanual grasps via k-means clustering and force-closure scoring, and (iv) validates stability with 6-axis gravity tests in IPC—yielding GRIP (1,200 objects, 100K grasps) with per-timestep contact, deformation, and stress fields.
paper_title: GRIP: A General Robotic Incremental Potential Contact Simulation Dataset for Unified Deformable-Rigid Coupled Grasping

problem: Lack of supervised tools to predict stress distributions in deformable objects during grasps, needed to prevent damage.
method: Train a physics-encoded GNN with cross-attention on GRIP’s stress annotations to predict pointwise von Mises stress from the object point cloud and gripper mesh at the grasp pose, achieving ~7–9% relative MAE and low KL divergence, validating the dataset’s utility for stress-aware manipulation.
paper_title: GRIP: A General Robotic Incremental Potential Contact Simulation Dataset for Unified Deformable-Rigid Coupled Grasping

problem: Robots lack an actionable, morphology-agnostic way to infer where and how to manipulate objects from raw images.
method: Introduce VRB: an affordance representation that predicts multi-modal contact point distributions (via K heatmap/GMM means) and post-contact trajectory waypoints in pixel space, which are projected to 3D for execution. This robot-centric c and τ abstraction avoids mimicking human motion and transfers across robots and tasks.
paper_title: Affordances from Human Videos as a Versatile Representation for Robotics

problem: Learning such affordances at scale from egocentric human videos is hard due to egomotion, humans in-frame causing domain shift, and limited supervision.
method: A robot-first training pipeline that mines labels from passive videos using a hand–object contact detector to find first-contact, skin-segmentation to extract contact points, and post-contact hand-box trajectories; compensates egomotion via homographies and maps labels to the earliest human-less frame to reduce domain shift. Train a ResNet-based deconv head to predict GMM means for contact heatmaps and a Transformer to predict relative trajectories; use local crops for generalization.
paper_title: Affordances from Human Videos as a Versatile Representation for Robotics

problem: How to leverage learned affordances to improve diverse real-world robot learning paradigms (imitation, exploration, goal-reaching, RL) efficiently.
method: Use VRB outputs to (a) guide offline data collection for k-NN and behavior cloning, (b) bootstrap reward-free exploration by ranking trajectories with an environment-change intrinsic reward and fitting elite distributions over (c, τ), (c) accelerate goal-conditioned learning by sampling trajectories closer to goal images, and (d) discretize continuous actions by GMM clustering over affordance outputs for DQN. Validated across 10 in-the-wild tasks, 4 environments, and 2 robot platforms with large performance gains over prior affordance models.
paper_title: Affordances from Human Videos as a Versatile Representation for Robotics

problem: Pretrained VLMs struggle to infer physical object concepts (e.g., material, fragility, deformability, contents) from static images, limiting their usefulness for manipulation planning without interaction data.
method: Create PHYSOBJECTS—an object-centric dataset of household items with 39.6K crowd-sourced and 417K automated annotations covering categorical concepts and pairwise preferences for continuous properties—and fine-tune InstructBLIP by casting each concept as VQA. For continuous concepts, convert yes/no answer likelihoods into a continuous score and train with a Bradley–Terry preference loss; for categorical concepts, use label likelihoods. The resulting PG-InstructBLIP substantially improves physical reasoning and transfers to held-out concepts.
paper_title: Physically Grounded Vision-Language Models for Robotic Manipulation

problem: LLM-based robotic planners lack reliable, scene-specific physical attributes of objects, causing failures on tasks requiring physical reasoning and safety constraints.
method: Integrate the physically grounded VLM into an interactive LLM planning loop: an object detector proposes bounding boxes; the LLM queries the VLM about physical concepts per object and consumes top-k answers with likelihoods to synthesize action plans from primitives. This composition markedly boosts plan accuracy on single- and multi-concept tasks and improves real-robot success rates.
paper_title: Physically Grounded Vision-Language Models for Robotic Manipulation
